faqs.answer: Get every extra written into your confirmation email before paying. Verbal promises from phone reps are frequently unknown to the front desk at check-in.

faqs.question: What's the biggest discount lever on vacation packages?
faqs.answer: Comparison shopping. Competition between brands creates spreads far larger than any phone negotiation, so comparing offers across brands and destinations is where the real savings live.

title: Why Do Hotel Rooms Still Have Bibles? The Whole Story
metaTitle: Why Do Hotel Rooms Still Have Bibles? | VacationDeals.to
metaDescription: The nightstand Bible comes from the Gideons, who've distributed free hotel Bibles since 1908. Here's who they are, why hotels said yes, and why it's fading.
author: The VacationDeals.to Team
bluf: That nightstand Bible was put there by the Gideons — a Christian association of traveling businessmen founded in the late 1890s who started placing free Bibles in hotel rooms in 1908. Hotels never bought them; the Gideons donate them, and hotels historically accepted because it cost nothing and guests expected it. The tradition is fading now, but the drawer habit dies hard.
heroImageAlt: An open nightstand drawer in a hotel room revealing a classic hardcover Bible
content:
Every hotel stay of my childhood included the same ritual: my brother would open the nightstand drawer, announce "Bible's here," and close it, like a tiny inspector certifying the room. We never asked WHY there was a Bible. It was just hotel furniture, like the ice bucket and the mystery chair no one sits in. Turns out the answer involves two traveling salesmen and a hotel with one room too few.

Bottom Line Up Front: Hotel Bibles come from Gideons International, an association of Christian traveling businessmen formed in the late 1890s after two salesmen shared a crowded hotel room. They began placing free Bibles in hotel rooms in 1908, and over the following century distributed them by the hundreds of millions worldwide. Hotels never pay for them — the Gideons donate and replace them — which is a big part of why the tradition stuck for so long.

Who Put the Bible in the Drawer?

The Gideons did — Gideons International, an evangelical association of traveling businessmen that formed in the late 1890s and started its hotel Bible program in 1908, placing donated Bibles in hotel rooms across America and eventually much of the world. The origin story, as the organization tells it, starts with two traveling salesmen forced to share a room at a crowded Wisconsin hotel in 1899. They got to talking, prayed together, and ended up founding an association for Christian commercial travelers — the 19th-century road warriors of they're day. The hotel Bible idea came a few years later: if members couldn't personally reach every traveler, the book could.

And the scale got genuinely staggering. Over more than a century, the Gideons say they've distributed Bibles in the billions across all their programs, with hotel rooms as the flagship. Industry folks generally treat the nightstand Bible as the most successful free-distribution program in hospitality history, which is a wierd category to have a champion in, but here we are.

Why Did Hotels Say Yes?

Because it was free, guests expected it, and saying yes was easier than saying no. The Gideons handle everything — supplying the Bibles, replacing worn ones through local volunteer chapters — so a hotel's total cost is drawer space. For most of the 20th century, American travelers broadly saw the Bible as a standard room amenity, somewhere between the phone book and the stationery. For a hotel manager in 1955, declining the free Bibles would of been the odd choice, not accepting them.

Two salesmen. One crowded hotel. A billion books.

Why Are Hotel Bibles Disappearing?

Because the traveler changed and the hotel changed. Surveys over the past decade or so have shown the share of hotels stocking religious materials declining noticably — boutique and design-forward brands often skip the drawer Bible entirely, some upscale properties offer religious texts "on request" instead, and newer brands courting younger travelers treat a bare drawer as a design statement. Add in properties that stock multiple faiths' texts or none, and the once-universal nightstand Bible is now more of a coin flip that varies by brand, region, and property age. The American South and drive-market family destinations — think Branson or Gatlinburg — remain the strongholds, which will suprise exactly no one who's been to Branson.

Property typeOdds of a drawer Bible
Classic chain hotel, US drive marketsHigh
Family resorts, Branson/GatlinburgVery high
Boutique/design hotels, big citiesLow
Vegas casino resortsMixed (yes, really — often present)
International all-inclusivesVaries widely by country

The Weird Trivia Layer

A few drawer facts to deploy at dinner: the association is named for Gideon, a biblical judge who won a battle with a deliberately tiny army — the founders liked the small-group-big-impact symbolism. The Bibles are placed by local volunteer chapters, not shipped from some central Bible warehouse. And contrary to hotel legend, the Gideons historically don't mind if a guest takes the Bible home; replacement is part of the program. Its the one thing in the room you can take without a checkout charge, which technically makes it the best amenity value in hospitality.

Pro Tip: If having a religious text in-room matters to you — or having none does — call the property before booking. Many hotels now keep texts of several faiths at the front desk on request, and front desks answer this question far more often than you'd think. It's a normal ask; make it.

It's worth pausing on what ELSE used to live in that drawer, because the Bible is the last survivor of a whole ecosystem. Phone books: extinct. Room service menus: migrated to QR codes. Stationery and postcards: mostly gone, mourned by approximately six people including me. The nightstand drawer of 1985 was a little civilization, and the Gideon Bible is its Rome — still standing while everything around it turned into a laminated card with a WiFi password. Whatever your beliefs, there's something impressive about being the only printed object in hospitality that survived the smartphone.

Inspector Brother, Vindicated

So the drawer Bible isn't hotel furniture after all — it's a 115+ year old volunteer logistics operation hiding in plain sight, slowly receding one renovation at a time. My brother was inspecting a piece of history. He'd be insufferable if he knew.

If this sent you down a nostalgia spiral for family road-trip hotels, the drawer-Bible heartland happens to overlap with some of the best cheap-vacation country in America — browse the current deals and check the destinations list. Open the drawer. Report your findings.

faqs.answer: No. The Gideons donate the Bibles and handle replacement at no cost to the hotel, which is a major reason the tradition persisted for over a century.

faqs.answer: Generally yes. The Gideons historically don't object to guests taking Bibles home — distribution is the point — and volunteer chapters replace them as part of the program.

faqs.answer: It's named for Gideon, the biblical figure who defeated a large army with a deliberately small force — symbolism the founders chose for a small group aiming at outsized impact.

faqs.answer: Many do on request. Some properties stock multiple faiths' texts at the front desk, so calling ahead is the reliable way to know what a specific hotel offers.

faqs.answer: Often yes, despite the reputation. Practice varies by property, but plenty of Vegas resorts have historically accepted Gideon Bibles like any other American hotel.

title: What Is a Resort Fee Actually For? An Honest Autopsy
metaTitle: What Is a Resort Fee Actually For? An Honest Autopsy | VacationDeals.to
metaDescription: Resort fees exist to make the advertised room rate look lower in search results. The "amenities" are cover. Here's the full autopsy and how to fight back.
author: The VacationDeals.to Team
bluf: A resort fee is for making the advertised room rate look cheaper than it really is. That's it — that's the autopsy. The fee gets unbundled from the rate so the hotel shows a lower price in search results, then collects the rest at the desk under the banner of "amenities" you mostly expected anyway. Regulators, including the FTC, have gone after the deceptive versions of this, and pricing rules keep tightening.
heroImageAlt: A hotel folio receipt under a magnifying glass revealing a mandatory resort fee line item
content:
The first time I got hit with a resort fee, the front desk clerk explained it covered "the pool, the gym, and local calls." It was 2015. I asked who was making local calls. She looked at the phone, then at me, and said, with the weariness of a war poet, "nobody." We understood each other completely. The fee stayed on the bill.

Bottom Line Up Front: The resort fee's true function is advertising optics: pull $30-50/night out of the room rate, and your hotel ranks cheaper in price-sorted search results than honest competitors. The "amenities" it supposedly covers — pool, gym, WiFi, bottled water — are things guests assume come with a resort anyway. Regulators like the FTC have pursued deceptive fee disclosure for years, and newer US pricing rules push total-price-up-front, but the fees themselves march on.

What Is a Resort Fee Actually Paying For?

Functionally, a resort fee pays for the hotel's placement in your search results — by carving a mandatory charge out of the advertised rate, the property displays a lower headline price than it actually charges, which wins the price-sort war against hotels that quote honestly. The industry calls the technique drip pricing, or unbundling: advertise $159, collect $159 plus a $45 "resort fee," and the amenities list is the costume the strategy wears in public. Pool access. Gym access. WiFi. A bottle of water with the emotional energy of an apology. Things that were, on any reasonable reading, allready part of being a resort.

Heres the tell: the fee is mandatory whether you use the amenities or not. Skip the pool, bring your own water, sweat exclusively outdoors — the fee survives. A charge you can't decline for services you didn't request isn't a service charge; it's rate, wearing a fake mustache.

Where Did Resort Fees Come From?

They crept in during the late 1990s and spread from resort destinations outward, with Las Vegas as the artform's undisputed capital — industry folks say fees at some Strip properties now run somewhere north of $50 a night, which on a cheap room can approach the room rate itself. Once a few big properties proved travelers would grumble-but-pay, competitors faced a brutal choice: adopt the fee and stay competitive in search rankings, or quote honestly and look expensive. Game theory did the rest. That's why fees cluster so heavily in Vegas, Orlando, and beach markets — anywhere search-comparison shopping dominates.

It's not a fee. It's rate in a fake mustache.

Hasn't Anyone Tried to Stop This?

Yes, repeatedly — the FTC has pursued hotels over deceptive fee disclosure, state attorneys general have sued major chains over hidden resort fees, and recent US rules have pushed mandatory total-price-up-front advertising so the full cost appears before checkout. The trendline is real: platforms and booking sites increasingly show fee-inclusive totals, and the sneakiest disclosure tricks keep getting sanded off. But notice what regulation targets — the HIDING, not the fee. A resort fee disclosed loudly and early is, legally speaking, mostly fine. So the fees adapt rather then die, like pricing cockroaches in a nice blazer.

What they say it's forWhat's really going on
"Pool and fitness center access"Was always included at any resort
"Enhanced WiFi"The same WiFi, rebranded
"Daily bottled water"Two bottles. Retail value: ~$2
"Local and toll-free calls"It is no longer 1997
Lower advertised rateThe actual product being purchased

How Do You Fight Back as a Traveler?

Four moves. One: compare TOTAL prices, never headline rates — a $139 room with a $45 fee loses to a $169 room with none, and price-sort rankings won't tell you that. Two: ask at booking, in writing, whether any mandatory fees apply; the answer binds better when its on your confirmation. Three: at checkout, if amenities were unavailable — pool closed, WiFi broken — ask for the fee to be reduced or removed; this works more often than people expect, because the fee's justification just collapsed. Four: favor properties and packages that don't play the game at all.

That last one is where vacpacks get interesting: preview packages are typically quoted as a total upfront price, and our listings surface known fees so the comparison is honest — check the current deals and the rate recap to see totals side by side. A $49-99 package with fees disclosed beats a "cheap" room that grows $50 a night at the desk.

Pro Tip: Before booking anywhere in a fee-heavy market, search the exact property name plus "resort fee" — properties disclose the current amount somewhere findable, and fee-tracking sites and forums keep score. Two minutes of homework converts every headline rate you see into a real number.

A last data point for the road: watch what happens when a market's fees get famous. Vegas resort fees became such a running joke that a few properties now advertise "no resort fees" as a headline amenity — proof that the fee got so bloated it looped back around into being a marketing opportunity for whoever drops it. When NOT charging a junk fee becomes a selling point, the autopsy writes itself.

Cause of Death: Optics

Autopsy complete. The resort fee exists because a lower fake number outranks a higher honest one, and everything else — the water bottles, the gym, my beloved local calls — is set dressing. It persists because it works, and it's slowly being domesticated by regulators who at least make it show its face.

Vote with your wallet: compare totals, favor honest pricing, and start with deals that show you the whole number. The under-$100 packages are a good first stop — what you see is, refreshingly, what you pay. Nobody will charge you for the local calls. Nobody makes them.

faqs.question: What does a resort fee actually cover?
faqs.answer: Nominally amenities like pool access, WiFi, gym use, and bottled water. Functionally, it lets the hotel advertise a lower room rate than it actually charges, since the fee is mandatory regardless of amenity use.

faqs.question: Why do hotels charge resort fees instead of raising room rates?
faqs.answer: Because the advertised rate is what wins price-sorted search comparisons. Moving $30-50 per night into a separate mandatory fee makes the hotel look cheaper than honest-quoting competitors.

faqs.question: Are resort fees legal?
faqs.answer: Yes, when properly disclosed. Regulators including the FTC and state attorneys general have gone after deceptive or hidden fee practices, and newer US rules require total prices to be shown up front.

faqs.question: Can you refuse to pay a resort fee?
faqs.answer: Not usually — mandatory fees are part of the booking terms. However, if paid amenities were unavailable during your stay, asking the desk to reduce or waive the fee succeeds fairly often.

faqs.question: Which destinations have the highest resort fees?
faqs.answer: Las Vegas leads, with fees at some Strip properties running north of $50 per night. Orlando and major beach markets also have widespread fees.

faqs.question: How do I find out a hotel's resort fee before booking?
faqs.answer: Search the property name plus "resort fee," check the rate details page before checkout, or ask at booking and have the answer added to your written confirmation.

faqs.question: Do vacation packages have resort fees?
faqs.answer: Some do, but preview packages are typically quoted as an upfront total, and reputable listings disclose known fees so you can compare true costs before booking.

faqs.question: What is drip pricing?
faqs.answer: Advertising a low headline price, then adding mandatory charges later in the purchase. Resort fees are the hotel industry's most prominent example, and regulators have increasingly targeted the practice.
